conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the official eligibility criteria to ensure I give you accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me start by asking you some questions to assess your eligibility.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I've got one child – my son Alex. He's 13 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know about Alex.

Now, do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. 

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or are you the only person responsible for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. I'm the only one responsible for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment. Is Alex currently in full-time education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's in secondary school at the moment, Year 8.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. One final question to ensure I have all the information I need:

Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or does he live with you full-time at your home in Bristol?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives with me full-time at home. He's not in hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Based on what you've told me, let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

You meet all the eligibility criteria:
- Alex is 13 years old (under 16)
- You live in the UK (Bristol)
- You are responsible for Alex and he lives with you full-time
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- He is not in hospital or care

You can proceed with applying for Child Benefit. Please note that only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child, and you've confirmed that no one else is claiming for Alex, so you should be able to claim without any issues.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), lives with you full-time in the UK (Bristol), is in full-time education (Year 8 at secondary school), you are responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, and he is not in hospital or care. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. All of these criteria are met for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Bristol, UK, and is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 13 years old. Alex lives full-time with the claimant at home, attends secondary school (Year 8), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. The claimant meets all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: they are responsible for a child under 16, they live with the child in the UK, and no one else is claiming for the same child.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), lives with you full-time in the UK (Bristol), is in full-time education (Year 8 at secondary school), you are responsible for him, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, and he is not in hospital or care. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You'll usually be responsible for a child if you live with them. All of these criteria are met for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Bristol, UK, and is responsible for one child, Alex, who is 13 years old. Alex lives full-time with the claimant at home, attends secondary school (Year 8), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. The claimant meets all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: they are responsible for a child under 16, they live with the child in the UK, and no one else is claiming for the same child.
